cards.definition: a family consisting of two parents and their dependent children living together
a family that includes relatives beyond the nuclear family — e.g. grandparents, aunts, uncles
a family with one parent and dependent children — the most common alternative to the nuclear family in the UK
a family formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together
a family with two parents of the same gender
the legal ending of a marriage — divorce rates have risen since the Divorce Reform Act 1969
living together as a couple without being married — increasingly common in the UK
a conservative political perspective that sees the traditional nuclear family as the ideal family structure
a sociological perspective that focuses on gender inequality — argues the family often oppresses women
unpaid work done in the home such as cooking, cleaning, and childcare — disproportionately done by women
a sociological perspective that sees society as a system where each part has a function — views the family as essential for stability
a sociological perspective based on the ideas of Karl Marx — argues society is shaped by economic inequality and class conflict
the first stage of learning society's norms and values — takes place mainly within the family
learning norms and values from institutions outside the family — e.g. school, media, peer groups
the unwritten rules about behaviour that are considered acceptable in society
the beliefs and principles shared by members of a society about what is important or desirable
the behaviours and expectations society associates with being male or female
Young and Willmott's idea that modern families have more equal sharing of domestic tasks between partners
the decline of religious influence in society — linked to declining rates of religious marriage
changes in the size and composition of a population — e.g. an ageing population, falling birth rates
the idea that success is based on ability and effort, not background or privilege
a system of social stratification based on economic factors such as income, wealth, and occupation
Bourdieu's idea that middle-class children have knowledge, attitudes, and skills that give them an advantage in education
lacking the physical resources needed to succeed in education — e.g. no quiet study space, poor diet, inability to afford equipment
the idea that teachers label students based on their class, gender, or ethnicity — affecting their self-belief and achievement
when a prediction comes true because people act on the label given to them — a key concept in education sociology
grouping students by perceived ability — can reinforce social class inequalities
the informal lessons schools teach about norms, values, and behaviour — e.g. obedience to authority, punctuality
the difference in educational achievement between boys and girls — girls outperform boys in most subjects at GCSE
differences in educational attainment between ethnic groups — Chinese and Indian students tend to outperform the national average in the UK
behaviour that breaks the law of society — defined by the state and can result in punishment
behaviour that breaks social norms but is not necessarily illegal — e.g. unusual dress, rude behaviour
the methods used by society to encourage conformity and discourage rule-breaking — formal and informal
control enforced by official institutions — e.g. police, courts, prison
control through social pressure and disapproval — e.g. family, peer groups, religion
the idea that crime occurs when people cannot achieve society's goals (e.g. wealth) through legitimate means
the idea that some groups develop alternative values that conflict with mainstream society — leading to delinquency
data on crime collected by police and the Home Office — critics argue they underrepresent the true level of crime
surveys asking people about crimes committed against them — the Crime Survey for England and Wales is the main example
crimes committed by people in professional, high-status occupations — e.g. fraud, tax evasion
data in numerical form — e.g. statistics, percentages, graphs. Associated with positivism
data in words, descriptions, or meanings — e.g. interview transcripts, observations. Associated with interpretivism
a set of written questions used to collect data from a large number of people
an interview where questions are fixed in advance — produces comparable data but limits depth of response
a free-flowing interview guided by the participant — produces rich, detailed data but harder to compare
the researcher joins a group and observes them from the inside — high validity but time-consuming and raises ethical issues
the extent to which a study produces the same results if repeated — quantitative methods tend to have higher reliability
the extent to which research measures what it claims to measure — qualitative methods tend to have higher validity
how well a sample reflects the wider population — affects whether findings can be generalised
the moral principles researchers must follow — including informed consent, confidentiality, and avoiding harm to participants
